content:
Let the horizontal line be \(A:y=0\) and let circle \(B\) have center \((0,R)\) with \(R=2008\).  A circle \(C_i\) of radius \(r_i\) sitting above the line and tangent to \(A\) must have center \((x_i,r_i)\) with \(x_i\) determined by tangency to \(B\).  Since it is outside \(B\) and tangent externally,

\[
\sqrt{(x_i-0)^2+(r_i-R)^2}\;=\;R+r_i
\;\Longrightarrow\;
x_i^2\;=\;(R+r_i)^2\;-\;(R-r_i)^2\;=\;4R\,r_i
\;\Rightarrow\;
x_i=2\sqrt{R\,r_i}\,.
\]

Next, requiring \(C_i\) to be externally tangent to its predecessor \(C_{i-1}\) gives
\[
\sqrt{(x_i-x_{i-1})^2+(r_i-r_{i-1})^2}\;=\;r_i+r_{i-1}\,.
\]
Substitute \(x_i=2\sqrt{R\,r_i}\) and \(x_{i-1}=2\sqrt{R\,r_{i-1}}\).  After some algebra one finds the convenient change of variable
\[
u_i=\sqrt{r_i}\,,\qquad
c=\frac1{\sqrt{R}}
\]
satisfies the linearized recursion
\[
v_i=c\,u_i,\quad
v_i=\frac{v_{i-1}}{1-v_{i-1}}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\frac1{v_i}=\frac1{v_{i-1}}-1
\]
so that
\[
\frac1{v_i}=\frac1{v_1}-(i-1)
=\sqrt{R}-(i-1),
\]
hence
\[
v_i=\frac1{\sqrt{R}-(i-1)},
\qquad
u_i=\frac{v_i}{c}
=\frac{\sqrt{R}}{\sqrt{R}-(i-1)},
\]
and
\[
r_i=u_i^2
=\frac{R}{\bigl(\sqrt{R}-(i-1)\bigr)^2}\;.
\]
Since the chain “blows up’’ exactly when the denominator \(\sqrt R-(i-1)\) hits zero, we must have
\[
\sqrt{R}-(n-1)>0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
n-1<\sqrt{R}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
n\le\lfloor\sqrt{2008}\rfloor+1
=\lfloor44.82\rfloor+1=45.
\]
Thus the largest possible \(n\) is \(\boxed{45}\).

message.content:
Let the horizontal line be \(A:y=0\) and let circle \(B\) have center \((0,R)\) with \(R=2008\).  A circle \(C_i\) of radius \(r_i\) sitting above the line and tangent to \(A\) must have center \((x_i,r_i)\) with \(x_i\) determined by tangency to \(B\).  Since it is outside \(B\) and tangent externally,

\[
\sqrt{(x_i-0)^2+(r_i-R)^2}\;=\;R+r_i
\;\Longrightarrow\;
x_i^2\;=\;(R+r_i)^2\;-\;(R-r_i)^2\;=\;4R\,r_i
\;\Rightarrow\;
x_i=2\sqrt{R\,r_i}\,.
\]

Next, requiring \(C_i\) to be externally tangent to its predecessor \(C_{i-1}\) gives
\[
\sqrt{(x_i-x_{i-1})^2+(r_i-r_{i-1})^2}\;=\;r_i+r_{i-1}\,.
\]
Substitute \(x_i=2\sqrt{R\,r_i}\) and \(x_{i-1}=2\sqrt{R\,r_{i-1}}\).  After some algebra one finds the convenient change of variable
\[
u_i=\sqrt{r_i}\,,\qquad
c=\frac1{\sqrt{R}}
\]
satisfies the linearized recursion
\[
v_i=c\,u_i,\quad
v_i=\frac{v_{i-1}}{1-v_{i-1}}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\frac1{v_i}=\frac1{v_{i-1}}-1
\]
so that
\[
\frac1{v_i}=\frac1{v_1}-(i-1)
=\sqrt{R}-(i-1),
\]

\[
v_i=\frac1{\sqrt{R}-(i-1)},
\qquad
u_i=\frac{v_i}{c}
=\frac{\sqrt{R}}{\sqrt{R}-(i-1)},
\]
and
\[
r_i=u_i^2
=\frac{R}{\bigl(\sqrt{R}-(i-1)\bigr)^2}\;.
\]
Since the chain “blows up’’ exactly when the denominator \(\sqrt R-(i-1)\) hits zero, we must have
\[
\sqrt{R}-(n-1)>0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
n-1<\sqrt{R}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
n\le\lfloor\sqrt{2008}\rfloor+1
=\lfloor44.82\rfloor+1=45.
\]
Thus the largest possible \(n\) is \(\boxed{45}\).
